blurb: A tidy clumping ornamental grass topped by long, soft purplish-pink 'bottlebrush' plumes that arch like a fountain from midsummer into fall — hardier and far better-behaved than the tender invasive fountain grasses.
water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant once established, needs sharp drainage
spread: Tidy clumping bunchgrass; can self-sow in the garden — deadhead to manage; not the tender invasive P. setaceum
seasons: Gray-green fountain of fine foliage spring–summer; pinkish-white to rose-purple bottlebrush plumes late summer into fall; fades to tan and stands into early winter
wildlife: Wind-pollinated grass — minor pollinator value; plumes offer cover, finch seed forage, and winter structure
toxic: No reported toxicity to people or pets; grown as an ornamental
origin: Central & Southwest Asia (Caucasus & Anatolia to Central Asia and India)
habitat: Dry rocky slopes, steppe and open stony ground

care.hardiness: A warm-season grass hardy to about USDA zone 6 as the straight species, and down to zone 5a in the tough cultivar 'Karley Rose', so it comes through Boulder's 5b–6a winters — the cultivars most reliably.[1][2] It greens up late in spring and blooms mid-to-late summer into fall; in an exposed, windy site a light winter mulch over the crown is cheap insurance the first year or two.[1]
care.planting: Set out container plants in late spring after the mid-May average last frost, once the soil has warmed — as a warm-season grass it establishes best in warm ground, not cold spring soil.[1][2] Water it in and keep it evenly moist through the first season while roots take hold; after that it needs far less. It grows well in a large, well-drained pot for the season, but a potted plant's roots are much less cold-hardy than the top, so a container left out over winter needs a big, sheltered pot (or a move to an unheated garage) rather than a small exposed one.[1]
care.sun: Full sun for the densest clump, the most plumes, and the best plume color; it takes light or part shade but flowers sparsely and flops in too much shade.[1][2] Our high-altitude sun and summer heat suit it well.[1]
care.soil: Average, well-drained soil is ideal — it does best in sandy or loamy ground and, importantly, dislikes heavy, wet clay, the one soil to avoid.[1] In Boulder's tight alkaline clay, plant it on a slope or a raised bed and work in grit and compost to sharpen drainage, or it can rot over a wet winter.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate. Water regularly the first season to establish; once mature it is genuinely drought-tolerant and gets by on occasional deep summer soaks.[1][2] Good drainage matters more than watering — cold, wet winter soil is what kills it, not dryness.[1]
care.spacing: Give each clump about 2–3 ft; mature plants reach roughly 2–3 ft tall and 2–4 ft wide, so a row of three drifts nicely without crowding.[1][2] Do not confuse it with the tender annual fountain grass (Pennisetum setaceum, crimson/African fountain grass), a warm-climate zone-9 plant grown as an annual here that is invasive in parts of California and the southwestern U.S.; this hardy Oriental species is not on Colorado's noxious-weed lists, but keep the two straight when buying.[2][3]
care.propagation: Increase it by DIVISION in spring as growth resumes — dig and split an established clump; named selections like 'Karley Rose' come true only this way.[1][2] It can also be raised from seed, but seedlings are variable and are exactly the volunteers you may want to prevent (see below).[2]
care.bloom: Soft, fluffy 'bottlebrush' plumes, roughly 8–12 in long, arch up above the foliage from mid/late summer into fall; they open pinkish-white with a pink cast and deepen to smoky mauve/rose-purple as they age.[1][2] They dry well and hold into early winter before shattering after hard freezes.[4]
care.feeding: Little to none — it performs well in lean, average soil, and rich feeding or high nitrogen only pushes floppy growth. A thin spring topdressing of compost is plenty.[1]
care.maintenance: One main chore: cut the whole clump to the ground in LATE WINTER, before new shoots push — it flushes late anyway, so cut too late and you'll be trimming around fresh green blades.[1] To keep it from self-sowing, shear the plumes off before the seed shatters in fall; the hardy fountain grasses can drop viable seed and volunteer around the garden, so deadheading is the simple control (a separate matter from the truly invasive tender P. setaceum).[2][4]
care.selfsow: Yes — as a hardy, seed-fertile fountain grass it can self-seed in the garden, and NC State flags it as having the potential to be weedy.[2] It is not a Colorado noxious weed, but pull unwanted volunteers while young and deadhead the plumes before frost if you want to prevent them; siting it away from wild or natural areas is the cautious choice.[2][4]
care.troubles: Largely trouble-free. Its real enemy is cold, wet, poorly drained soil, which rots the crown over winter — the reason to give it sharp drainage here.[1] Foliar rust or leaf spot can appear in wet, humid spells (uncommon in dry Boulder), and deer and rabbits generally leave it alone.[2]
care.companions: A fine-textured, arching accent among stiffer, mounding perennials — pair it with catmint, salvia, coneflower, black-eyed Susan, sedum, and yarrow for a border that reads well from summer into winter.[2]

edible.summary: Not edible — an ornamental grass grown for its foliage and plumes, with no culinary use.[2]
edible.caution: No part of Oriental fountain grass is used as food and there are no documented edible uses; it is grown purely as an ornamental. It is not reported as toxic to people or pets, but the barbed, awned seedheads can be a mechanical irritant to grazing animals, and the absence of any food documentation means it should simply be treated as inedible — don't eat it.[2][3]
